text.title: Change Control Lifecycle
text.summary: The six-step change control process: receive, log, analyze impact, decide with tiered authority, implement, and close with documentation.

section.title: Step 1: Receive and Log

rule.statement: Capture every change request in writing with a unique ID before any analysis or work begins; verbal requests are not change requests.
rule.rationale: Undocumented changes become disputed scope — the requester remembers agreement, the team remembers refusal, and neither has evidence. A CR ID creates a paper trail from the moment of request.

section.p: Required fields at receipt: requester name and role, date submitted, plain-language description of the change, business justification, and urgency.
CR status lifecycle: Submitted → Under Review → Approved / Rejected / Deferred → Implemented (if approved) → Closed.

section.title: Step 2: Impact Analysis

rule.statement: Every impact analysis must include a "do nothing" option; omitting it prevents the explicit cost-of-inaction reasoning that justifies or rejects the change.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline option, decision makers compare change variants against each other rather than against the status quo, which inflates the perceived value of every change.

section.p: Impact areas to assess for every CR:

list.item: Scope — which features or WBS elements are affected.
Schedule — additional days; which milestones move.
Cost — additional budget; which line items are affected.
Quality — impact on standards, testing, technical debt.
Risk — new risks introduced; existing risks affected.
Resources — who must work on it; opportunity cost to other work.
Test impact — regression and integration testing required (explicitly, not bundled into "Quality").

section.reference: Change request form with impact analysis

rule.statement: Define decision-authority thresholds in writing at kickoff, post them publicly, and do not revise them without sponsor agreement; thresholds that drift upward make every change a "minor" change.
rule.rationale: Without written thresholds, PMs informally expand their own authority over time ("it's only 2 days, I'll approve it myself"), which bypasses the sponsor and sponsor approval stops being meaningful.

list.item: Minor (e.g., less than 1 day, less than $500) — PM approves.
Major (e.g., more than 5 days, more than $5,000) — CCB approves.

rule.statement: Bundle minor CRs into a weekly "minor changes" approval batch to maintain cadence without per-request ceremony; present the aggregate schedule and cost impact, not individual items.
rule.rationale: Per-item approval for minor changes produces either rubber-stamping (no real bar) or approval fatigue; aggregate presentation makes the cumulative cost visible and triggers escalation when it crosses a threshold.

section.title: Step 4: Implement and Close

rule.statement: Tie every approved CR to a baseline-update commit (WBS, schedule, budget) in the same PR; scope changes without corresponding document updates create drift between the plan and the baseline.
rule.rationale: A CR logged as "approved" but not reflected in the scope baseline and schedule produces a team working to the new scope while reporting against the old baseline — a measurement error masquerading as performance.

section.reference: Change register for tracking all CRs
Script to sum cumulative schedule/cost impact of approved CRs
